content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactually Primed Prompting: Reducing Social Bias in LLM Outputs via Dual-Context Interventions",
  "problem": "Large language models often reinforce social biases and stereotypes in their outputs, even when prompts are neutral. Existing prompting techniques insufficiently address implicit bias propagation.",
  "motivation": "Mitigating bias in LLMs is increasingly urgent as they are integrated into high-stakes applications. Prompting methods offer a non-intrusive, model-agnostic way to steer outputs, but current approaches lack robustness against subtle or context-dependent biases. Developing more effective prompting strategies is crucial for equitable AI deployment.",
  "method": "We propose Counterfactually Primed Prompting (CPP), a technique where each input prompt is augmented with a dual-context: (1) the original prompt and (2) a counterfactual scenario where sensitive attributes (e.g., gender, race) are swapped or neutralized. The LLM is instructed to generate its response conditioned on both contexts and explicitly reconcile or compare them, thereby surfacing and reducing context-driven bias. CPP can be implemented as a prompt template or via in-context examples demonstrating balanced outputs.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CPP on bias-sensitive benchmarks such as StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs, comparing against standard prompting, instruction-based prompts, and anti-bias prompt templates. Bias metrics (e.g., stereotype score, log-prob gap) and response quality (fluency/accuracy) will be measured. Human evaluation will assess stereotype prevalence and output fairness."
}
